records.nearby_text_before: Key idea in statistics: explain the variation To do this we need to express scientifically the variation in the data as observations of random variables and use a statistical model to explain it (at least partially). Statistical analysis requires data, which may be viewed as an observed sample from a population.
records.text_raw: The data X 1 , … , Xn are considered as n , usually independent and identically distributed, random variables with a common population distribution/model. This population can be specified as a formula with either its cumulative distribution function (cdf) F ( x ) or its probability density function (pdf) f ( x ).
records.text_cleaned: The data $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ are considered as $n$, usually independent and identically distributed, random variables with a common population distribution/model. This population can be specified as a formula with either its cumulative distribution function (cdf) $F(x)$ or its probability density function (pdf) $f(x)$.

records.nearby_text_before: Validation Set Approach vs. LOOCV LOOCV has far less bias and, therefore, tends not to overestimate the test error rate. Performing LOOCV multiple times always yields the same results because there is no randomness in the training/validation set splits.
records.text_raw: LOOCV is computationally intensive because the model has to be fit n times. However, there is a shortcut with OLS linear or polynomial regression (where hi is the leverage):
records.text_cleaned: LOOCV is computationally intensive because the model has to be fit n times. However, there is a shortcut with OLS linear or polynomial regression (where h_i is the leverage):

records.nearby_text_before: K -Fold Cross-Validation (cont.) We use this method because LOOCV is computationally intensive/slow. We randomly divide the data set of into K folds (typically K = 5 or 10).
records.nearby_text_after: By averaging the K estimates of the test error, we get an estimated validation (test) error rate for new observations.
records.text_raw: One of the folds is treated as a test set, and the method is fit on the remaining K -1 folds. The MSPE is computed on the observations in the held-out fold. The process is repeated K times, taking out a different part each time.
records.text_cleaned: One of the folds is treated as a test set, and the method is fit on the remaining $K-1$ folds. The MSPE is computed on the observations in the held-out fold. The process is repeated $K$ times, taking out a different part each time.

records.nearby_text_before: K -Fold Cross-Validation (cont.)

records.nearby_text_before: Which is better, LOOCV or K -fold CV? -LOOCV is more computationally intensive than K -fold CV -From the perspective of bias reduction, LOOCV is preferred to K -fold CV (when K < n )
records.nearby_text_after: -Thus, we see the bias-variance trade-off between the two resampling methods We tend to use K -fold CV with K = 5 or K = 10, as these values have been shown empirically to yield test error rate estimates that suffer neither from excessively high bias nor from very high variance
records.text_raw: -However, LOOCV has higher variance than K -fold CV (when K < n )
records.text_cleaned: -However, LOOCV has higher variance than K-fold CV (when K < n)

records.nearby_text_before: Cross-Validation on Classification Problems We will cover classification problems in more detail later in the course, but we briefly show how CV can be used when Y is qualitative (categorical) as opposed to quantitative. Here, rather than use MSE to quantify test error, we instead use the number of misclassified observation.
records.nearby_text_after: We use CV as follows: -Divide data into K folds; hold-out one part and fit using the remaining data (compute error rate on hold-out data); repeat K times. -CV Error Rate: average over the K errors we have computed

records.text_cleaned: LOOCV Error Rate: CV(n) = 1/n \sum_{i=1}^n Err_i, where Err_i = I(Y_i \ne \hat{Y}_i)

records.nearby_text_before: The Bootstrap: Overview (cont.)
records.nearby_text_after: The basic idea is to randomly draw datasets with replacement from the training data, each sample the same size as the original training set. This is done B times, producing B bootstrap datasets. Then we refit the model to each of the bootstrap datasets, and examine the behavior of the fits over the B replications.

records.text_raw: A movie theater owner likes to keep a very close eye on how much popcorn goes into each bag sold, so he knows that the amount is normally distributed and the average bag has 8 cups of popcorn and that this varies a little bit, about half a cup. That is, the population mean should be μ = 8.00 and the known population standard deviation is σ = 0.50. The owner wants to make sure that the newest employee is filling bags correctly, so over the course of a week he randomly assesses 25 bags filled by the employee to test for a difference ( n = 25). He doesn't want bags overfilled or under filled, so he looks for differences in both directions.
records.text_cleaned: A movie theater owner likes to keep a very close eye on how much popcorn goes into each bag sold, so he knows that the amount is normally distributed and the average bag has 8 cups of popcorn and that this varies a little bit, about half a cup. That is, the population mean should be μ = 8.00 and the known population standard deviation is σ = 0.50. The owner wants to make sure that the newest employee is filling bags correctly, so over the course of a week he randomly assesses 25 bags filled by the employee to test for a difference (n = 25). He doesn't want bags overfilled or under filled, so he looks for differences in both directions.

records.nearby_text_before: Permutation Test Example The approximate permutation test procedure is illustrated below with the two-sample t statistic.
records.text_raw: R = 999 # number of replications of the test statistic z = c(x, y) # vectorized data from the two samples K <- 1:26 reps <- numeric(R); t0 <- t.test(x, y, var.equal = T)$statistic #number of replicates #pooled sample #storage for replicates for (i in 1:R) { #generate indices k for the first sample k <- sample(K, size = 14, replace = FALSE) x1 <- z[k] y1 <- z[-k] #complement of x1 reps[i] <- t.test(x1, y1, var.equal = T)$statistic } p <- mean(c(t0, reps) >= t0) > p [1] 0.103
records.text_cleaned: R = 999 # number of replications of the test statistic z = c(x, y) # vectorized data from the two samples K <- 1:26 reps <- numeric(R); t0 <- t.test(x, y, var.equal = T)$statistic #number of replicates #pooled sample #storage for replicates for (i in 1:R) { #generate indices k for the first sample k <- sample(K, size = 14, replace = FALSE) x1 <- z[k] y1 <- z[-k] #complement of x1 reps[i] <- t.test(x1, y1, var.equal = T)$statistic } p <- mean(c(t0, reps) >= t0) > p [1] 0.103

records.nearby_text_before: Correlation is commonplace -... Per capita consumption of Butter in the US · Source: USDA ·- BLS estimate of epidemiologists in Colorado · Source: Bureau of Larbor
records.nearby_text_after: http://www.tylervigen.com/spurious-correlations As butter consumption in Colorado increased, so did the demand for larger sample sizes in epidemiological studies. This led to a surge in funding for public health research, prompting a migration of epidemiologists to the state, lured by the opportunity to conduct margarineally significant studies and spread their knowledge like, well, butter. Data dredging: They have 25,153 variables in a database. They compare all these variables against each other to find ones that randomly match up. That's 632,673,409 correlation calculations! This is called 'data dredging.'

records.nearby_text_before: Ordinary least squares (OLS) regression works well in many real-world applications. Recall, in OLS, we fit a linear model of the form: We find estimates for the betas that minimize the sum of squared errors (SSE): The solution to the problem can be expressed as:
records.text_raw: where the design matrix X is of size n by p +1 because the first column is all ones for the intercept.
records.text_cleaned: where the design matrix X is of size n by p + 1 because the first column is all ones for the intercept.

records.nearby_text_before: Subset selection. Instead of fitting OLS on all possible predictor variables, we first eliminate some by setting their coefficients to zero and then fit OLS on the remaining predictors. Regularization/shrinkage. The model is fit on all p predictors, but the magnitudes of the coefficients are shrunk towards zero. These methods work by adding a penalty to the loss function based on the magnitude of the regression coefficients. One method of regularization, called the lasso, uses SSE plus a regularization term as the loss function: SSE: usual error term for OLS regression
records.nearby_text_after: Partial Least Squares (PLS). A supervised alternative to PCR.
records.section_title: Alternatives to OLS for Linear Models
records.text_raw: Dimension reduction. Here we project the p predictors down into an M -dimensional space, where M < p , via linear combinations of variables, or directions . We then use the smaller set of m projections as the predictors to be fit by OLS.
records.text_cleaned: Dimension reduction. Here we project the p predictors down into an M-dimensional space, where M < p, via linear combinations of variables, or directions. We then use the smaller set of m projections as the predictors to be fit by OLS.

records.nearby_text_before: Subset Selection Best subset selection involves fitting the OLS model for every possible combination of the p predictors and picking the one that is best. This is a brute force method where we must fit all possible models. Assume p predictors. The number of possible combinations (order of the predictors doesn't matter) are
records.nearby_text_after: The sum of all these combinations is 2 p.

Pick the single best model from M 0, …, Mp using cross-validation prediction error, or some other measure of model fit such as the AIC or BIC.

records.nearby_text_before: Probabilistic selection: AIC and BIC The Akaike Information Criterion, or AIC for short, is a method for scoring and selecting a model. It is named for the developer of the method, Hirotugu Akaike, and may be shown to have a basis in information theory and frequentist-based inference. BIC is a similar concept.
records.nearby_text_after: Here there is a penalty for magnitude of the LL; smaller is better The likelihood (assuming normally distributed errors) for multiple linear regression is Here there is a penalty for more complexity in the model.
records.text_raw: The AIC and BIC are both based on -2 times the maximized value of the loglikelihood (-2 LL ); k is the number of parameters and LL is the log-likelihood value at the MLE:
records.text_cleaned: The AIC and BIC are both based on -2 times the maximized value of the loglikelihood (-2 LL); k is the number of parameters and LL is the log-likelihood value at the MLE:

records.nearby_text_before: Probabilistic selection: AIC and BIC The Akaike Information Criterion, or AIC for short, is a method for scoring and selecting a model. It is named for the developer of the method, Hirotugu Akaike, and may be shown to have a basis in information theory and frequentist-based inference. BIC is a similar concept. The AIC and BIC are both based on -2 times the maximized value of the loglikelihood (-2 LL ); k is the number of parameters and LL is the log-likelihood value at the MLE:
records.nearby_text_after: The likelihood (assuming normally distributed errors) for multiple linear regression is Here there is a penalty for more complexity in the model. The score, as defined above, is minimized, e.g. the model with the lowest AIC is selected.
records.text_raw: Here there is a penalty for magnitude of the LL ; smaller is better
records.text_cleaned: Here there is a penalty for magnitude of the LL; smaller is better

records.nearby_text_before: As you see from their definitions, AIC and BIC are similarly constructed, with the essential difference being the term multiplied by k, the number of estimated coefficients.
records.nearby_text_after: The AIC tries to select the model that most adequately describes an unknown, high dimensional reality. Because the AIC is somewhat more permissive of model complexity than the BIC, it may be preferred for prediction. When the intent is to create a model for explanation, BIC may be preferred because it will tend to produce a simpler (i.e., more easily interpretable) model.
records.text_raw: As a result (2 vs. log(n)), the BIC tends to penalize model complexity more heavily than the AIC.
records.text_cleaned: As a result (2 vs. log(n)), the BIC tends to penalize model complexity more heavily than the AIC.

records.nearby_text_before: Best Subset and Computational Efficiency Best subset selection is very computationally demanding.
records.nearby_text_after: Whereas forward selection (described next) will require fitting 1 + 1 + 2 + 3 + … + 29 + 30 = p ( p + 1)/2 + 1 = 451 models. That said, the ease of fitting Gaussian linear models make even large (i.e., 30+ covariates) problems tractable within a few seconds. This is possible in part due to advances in computational algorithms over the last decades. The problem of computational efficiency is quickly made worse, however, by using other models that require iterative numerical methods for solving.
records.text_raw: For p = 30, for example, best subset will require fitting 2 30 = 1,073,741,824 models.
records.text_cleaned: For p = 30, for example, best subset will require fitting 2^30 = 1,073,741,824 models.

records.nearby_text_before: Best Subset and Computational Efficiency Best subset selection is very computationally demanding. For p = 30, for example, best subset will require fitting 2 30 = 1,073,741,824 models.
records.nearby_text_after: That said, the ease of fitting Gaussian linear models make even large (i.e., 30+ covariates) problems tractable within a few seconds. This is possible in part due to advances in computational algorithms over the last decades. The problem of computational efficiency is quickly made worse, however, by using other models that require iterative numerical methods for solving. For example, with Gaussian linear models (which can be solved analytically), a best subset search with 11 predictors takes less than a second.

records.nearby_text_before: Logistic Regression Example: Some Results
records.nearby_text_after: Equation: log(odds of subsrcibing) = -2.081 + 0.00003958*balance Interpretation of the slope: If the balance increases by 1 euro, then the log of the odds (of subscribing) will increase by 0.00003958 units.
records.text_raw: > logit0 = glm(y ~ balance, data = bank, family = "binomial") > summary(logit0) Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) (Intercept) -2.081e+00 1.595e-02 -130.50 <2e-16 *** balance 3.958e-05 3.840e-06 10.31 <2e-16 ***
records.text_cleaned: > logit0 = glm(y ~ balance, data = bank, family = "binomial") > summary(logit0) Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) (Intercept) -2.081e+00 1.595e-02 -130.50 <2e-16 *** balance 3.958e-05 3.840e-06 10.31 <2e-16 ***

records.nearby_text_before: Logistic Regression Example: Some Results > logit0 = glm(y ~ balance, data = bank, family = "binomial") > summary(logit0) Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) (Intercept) -2.081e+00 1.595e-02 -130.50 <2e-16 *** balance 3.958e-05 3.840e-06 10.31 <2e-16 ***
records.nearby_text_after: Interpretation of the slope: If the balance increases by 1 euro, then the log of the odds (of subscribing) will increase by 0.00003958 units. Equivalent (and better!): the odds of subscribing will be multiplied by e 0.00003958 = 1.00004 for each extra 1 euro of balance.

records.nearby_text_before: The deviance for a GLM m is defined as It measures the difference of the fitted generalized linear model with respect to a perfect model for the sample. This perfect model, known as the saturated model, is the model that perfectly fits the data, in the sense that the fitted responses equal the observed responses.
records.text_raw: For a linear model, deviance is the sum of squared errors (SSE) and null deviance D0 is the total sum of squares (SST).

records.nearby_text_after: Empirical evidence shows that with count data the effects of predictors are often multiplicative rather than additive. That is, one typically observes small effects for small counts, and large effects for large counts.
records.text_raw: Like the logistic regression, exp( b j ) represents the multiplicative effect of the j th predictor on the mean, keeping the other predictors fixed. That is, increasing Xj by one unit multiplies the mean of the response variable by a factor of exp( b j ).
records.text_cleaned: Like the logistic regression, exp(b_j) represents the multiplicative effect of the jth predictor on the mean, keeping the other predictors fixed. That is, increasing Xj by one unit multiplies the mean of the response variable by a factor of exp(b_j).

records.nearby_text_before: Interpretation of the Poisson Regression parameters
records.nearby_text_after: for each extra $1 increase in price, log[E( Y )] decreases by 0.084 units, keeping other predictors fixed. That is, expected quantity sold will multiply by exp(-0.084) = 0.92. This means there is 8% decrease in demand for every $1 increase in price (as per basic economics theory). Coefficient for D 1 (lowest price in its category), that is -0.912, with exp(-0.912) = 0.4017119: there is 59.8% decrease in demand if wine is the cheapest in its category and all other predictors are the same.

records.nearby_text_before: The survival function (or curve) is defined as This decreasing function quantifies the probability of surviving past time t. For example, suppose that a company is interested in modeling customer churn. Let T represent the time that a customer cancels a subscription to the company's service.
records.text_raw: Then S ( t ) represents the probability that a customer cancels later than time t . The larger the value of S ( t ), the less likely that the customer will cancel before time t .
records.text_cleaned: Then S(t) represents the probability that a customer cancels later than time t. The larger the value of S(t), the less likely that the customer will cancel before time t.

records.nearby_text_after: It is tempting to simply compute the proportion of patients who are known to have survived past 20 months, that is, the proportion of patients for whom Y > 20. This turns out to be 48 / 88, or approximately 55%. However, this does not seem quite right: 17 of the 40 patients who did not survive to 20 months were censored, and this analysis implicitly assumes they died before 20 months. Hence it is probably an underestimate.

records.nearby_text_before: Suppose we'd like to estimate S (20) = Pr( T > 20), the probability that a patient survives for more than 20 months, It is tempting to simply compute the proportion of patients who are known to have survived past 20 months, that is, the proportion of patients for whom Y > 20. This turns out to be 48 / 88, or approximately 55%.
records.text_raw: However, this does not seem quite right: 17 of the 40 patients who did not survive to 20 months were censored, and this analysis implicitly assumes they died before 20 months. Hence it is probably an underestimate.

records.nearby_text_after: However, this is not quite right either, since it amounts to completely ignoring the patients who were censored before time t = 20, even though the time at which they are censored is potentially informative. records.nearby_text_before: Cox's Proportional Hazards Model
records.nearby_text_after: The name proportional hazards arises from the fact that the hazard function for an individual with feature vector xi is some unknown function h 0( t ) times the factor 𝑒 σ 𝑗=1 𝑝 𝑥𝑖𝑗 𝛽 𝑗. The quantity 𝑒 σ 𝑗=1 𝑝 𝑥𝑖𝑗 𝛽 𝑗 is called the relative risk for the feature vector xi = ( xi 1,..., xip ), relative to that for the feature vector xi = (0,..., 0).
records.text_raw: The proportional hazards assumption states that: ℎ 𝑡 𝑥𝑖 = ℎ0 𝑡 𝑒 σ 𝑗=1 𝑝 𝑥𝑖𝑗 𝛽 𝑗 where h 0( t ) ≥ 0 is an unspecified function, known as the baseline hazard . It is the hazard function for an individual with features xi 1 = · = xip = 0.
records.text_cleaned: The proportional hazards assumption states that: $h(t\mid x_i)=h_0(t)e^{\sum_{j=1}^p x_{ij}\beta_j}$ where $h_0(t)\ge 0$ is an unspecified function, known as the baseline hazard. It is the hazard function for an individual with features $x_{i1}=\cdots=x_{ip}=0$.

records.nearby_text_before: Partial Likelihood -Computation To estimate β, we simply maximize the partial likelihood with respect to β. As is the case for logistic regression, no closed-form solution is available, and so iterative algorithms are required. In addition to estimating β, we can also obtain other model outputs, like those in least squares regression and logistic regression.
records.text_raw: For example, we can obtain p-values corresponding to particular null hypotheses (e.g. H 0 : β j = 0), as well as estimated standard errors and confidence intervals associated with the coefficients.

records.nearby_text_after: We have assumed that there are no tied failure times. records.nearby_text_before: Until now, all models assumed i.i.d. random sample framework
records.nearby_text_after: This can be done with two approaches: Markov chains or time series Markov models provide a very rich set of tools for handling dependence Prof. Andrei A. Markov (1856-1922), published his result in 1906.
records.text_raw: We now consider a dependence structure between the observations X 1 , … , Xn
records.text_cleaned: We now consider a dependence structure between the observations $X_1, \ldots, X_n$

records.nearby_text_after: The probabilities of moving from state to state are constant and independent of the past behavior -this property of the system is called the Markov property. That is, We assume that a transition -picking up and dropping off a fare -occurs each time the system is observed, and that observations occur at regular intervals. Systems with these characteristics are called Markov chains or Markov processes (when time is continuous).
records.text_raw: If the location of the taxi at time n is denoted by Xn , then the sequence X 1 , X 2 , … consists of dependent variables, and can be modeled with a Markov chain. The values the random variable Xn can take, are known as states of the chain.
records.text_cleaned: If the location of the taxi at time n is denoted by Xn, then the sequence X1, X2, … consists of dependent variables, and can be modeled with a Markov chain. The values the random variable Xn can take are known as states of the chain.

records.nearby_text_before: Transition Matrix We can create a square matrix, P, called the transition matrix, by constructing rows for the probabilities going from Southside and Northside as well.
records.text_raw: An entry Pij of this matrix is the probability of a transition from region i to region j . For example, p32 , is the probability of a fare that originates in Northside going to Southside. (Note the sum of entries across rows of P .)
records.text_cleaned: An entry $P_{ij}$ of this matrix is the probability of a transition from region $i$ to region $j$. For example, $P_{32}$ is the probability of a fare that originates in Northside going to Southside. (Note the sum of entries across rows of $P$.)
